message.content: <local-command-caveat>Caveat: The messages below were generated by the user while running local commands. DO NOT respond to these messages or otherwise consider them in your response unless the user explicitly asks you to.</local-command-caveat>

can you websearch an aspect of rust build hygene?  I want to run a code generator before every build: will a datestamp change on the file cause rust to have to rebuild?  Or if codegen doesn't change file, will it avoid rebuilds that involve the file directly?

## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the current diff and apply the fixes — equivalent to /code-review --fix.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

aiTitle: Rust build caching with generated files

input.query: Cargo rebuild based on file mtime or content hash unchanged file timestamp
cargo build.rs codegen rerun-if-changed unchanged content avoid rebuild mtime

content.content:
Web search results for query: "Cargo rebuild based on file mtime or content hash unchanged file timestamp"

Links: [{"title":"[WIP] mtime+content tracking by gilescope · Pull Request #8623 · rust-lang/cargo","url":"https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/pull/8623"},{"title":"fingerprint.rs.html -- source","url":"https://docs.rs/cargo/0.39.0/src/cargo/core/compiler/fingerprint.rs.html"},{"title":"Rebuild on mid build file modification by Eh2406 · Pull Request #6484 · rust-lang/cargo","url":"https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/pull/6484"},{"title":"cargo build does not rebuild if a source file was modified during a build · Issue #2426 · rust-lang/cargo","url":"https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/2426"},{"title":"(Option to) Fingerprint by file contents instead of mtime #6529","url":"https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/6529"},{"title":"Build Scripts - The Cargo Book","url":"https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/reference/build-scripts.html"},{"title":"Cargo, Docker and mtime | François Best","url":"https://francoisbest.com/posts/2021/cargo-docker-mtime"},{"title":"Locale checks file mtime but this is tricky if the translation files are copied to an image by Docker use a hash of the file instead [#3575678] | Drupal.org","url":"https://www.drupal.org/project/drupal/issues/3575678"},{"title":"Cargo Cache Invalidation | zudo-tauri-wisdom","url":"https://takazudomodular.com/pj/zudo-tauri/docs/deployment/cargo-cache/"},{"title":"cargo-hold - crates.io: Rust Package Registry","url":"https://crates.io/crates/cargo-hold"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding Cargo rebuild behavior with file mtime and content hashing:

## Current Approach

Cargo does not use a hash-based mechanism to check for modified source files, but keeps track of file modification times instead. Cargo only uses the filesystem last-modified "mtime" timestamp to determine if the file has changed.

## The Problem with mtime-Only Tracking

This approach has significant limitations. When building code on Travis, the actual code in a workspace builds every time, even though much of it hasn't changed, because Travis makes a new clone of the git repo, which doesn't preserve mtimes, potentially adding about 5 minutes to every Travis run.

## Hash-Based Solution Efforts

An unstable option `-Zhash-tracking` was proposed that uses content hashes and file sizes as well as mtimes to prevent compile cascades when mtimes are messed with but the content is unchanged. This approach is almost zero cost when mtimes are up-to-date, with cost only incurred when mtime checks fail and typically the filesize check means a more expensive hash check can be skipped.

## Current Recommendations

For practical workarounds, tools like `cargo-hold` have emerged that scan for changes by computing BLAKE3 hashes of files and restore timestamps intelligently, with unchanged files restored to their original timestamps from metadata and modified files given a new monotonic timestamp.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding Cargo rebuild behavior with file mtime and content hashing:

## Current Approach

Cargo does not use a hash-based mechanism to check for modified source files, but keeps track of file modification times instead. Cargo only uses the filesystem last-modified "mtime" timestamp to determine if the file has changed.

## The Problem with mtime-Only Tracking

This approach has significant limitations. When building code on Travis, the actual code in a workspace builds every time, even though much of it hasn't changed, because Travis makes a new clone of the git repo, which doesn't preserve mtimes, potentially adding about 5 minutes to every Travis run.

## Hash-Based Solution Efforts

An unstable option `-Zhash-tracking` was proposed that uses content hashes and file sizes as well as mtimes to prevent compile cascades when mtimes are messed with but the content is unchanged. This approach is almost zero cost when mtimes are up-to-date, with cost only incurred when mtime checks fail and typically the filesize check means a more expensive hash check can be skipped.

## Current Recommendations

For practical workarounds, tools like `cargo-hold` have emerged that scan for changes by computing BLAKE3 hashes of files and restore timestamps intelligently, with unchanged files restored to their original timestamps from metadata and modified files given a new monotonic timestamp.

content.content:
Web search results for query: "cargo build.rs codegen rerun-if-changed unchanged content avoid rebuild mtime"

Links: [{"title":"rerun_if_changed in cargo_emit - Rust","url":"https://docs.rs/cargo-emit/latest/cargo_emit/macro.rerun_if_changed.html"},{"title":"rerun_if_changed in cargo_build - Rust","url":"https://docs.rs/cargo-build/latest/cargo_build/fn.rerun_if_changed.html"},{"title":"Build Scripts - The Cargo Book","url":"https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/reference/build-scripts.html"},{"title":"\"cargo::rerun-if-changed\" is not working. build.rs is run every time. · Issue #15716 · rust-lang/cargo","url":"https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/15716"},{"title":"Build script rerun-if changes break rebuild detection. · Issue #7362 · rust-lang/cargo","url":"https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/7362"},{"title":"Build Scripts - The Cargo Book","url":"https://web.mit.edu/rust-lang_v1.25/arch/amd64_ubuntu1404/share/doc/rust/html/cargo/reference/build-scripts.html"},{"title":"rerun_if_changed.rs - source","url":"https://docs.rs/cargo-emit/latest/src/cargo_emit/rerun_if_changed.rs.html"}]

I found search results related to your query about Cargo's `rerun-if-changed` directive and build script behavior. Here's what the documentation shows:

## How rerun-if-changed Works

The `rerun-if-changed` instruction tells Cargo to re-run the build script if the file at the given path has changed. Currently, Cargo only uses the filesystem last-modified "mtime" timestamp to determine if the file has changed, and it compares against an internal cached timestamp of when the build script last ran.

## Avoiding Unnecessary Rebuilds

If the build script inherently does not need to re-run under any circumstance, then emitting `cargo::rerun-if-changed=build.rs` is a simple way to prevent it from being re-run. If these instructions are emitted, then Cargo will only re-run the script if the given value has changed.

## Key Considerations

If the path points to a directory, it will scan the entire directory for any modifications. Note that Cargo does not clean or reset OUT_DIR between builds. The contents of this directory may persist across rebuilds, even if the build script is re-run. This behavior is intentional to support incremental builds, such as native code compilation.

The search results also reveal there have been some reported issues with `rerun-if-changed` behavior in recent Rust versions, though these appear to be edge cases related to changing which directives are emitted between runs rather than problems with unchanged file content detection.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

## How rerun-if-changed Works

The `rerun-if-changed` instruction tells Cargo to re-run the build script if the file at the given path has changed. Currently, Cargo only uses the filesystem last-modified "mtime" timestamp to determine if the file has changed, and it compares against an internal cached timestamp of when the build script last ran.

## Avoiding Unnecessary Rebuilds

If the build script inherently does not need to re-run under any circumstance, then emitting `cargo::rerun-if-changed=build.rs` is a simple way to prevent it from being re-run. If these instructions are emitted, then Cargo will only re-run the script if the given value has changed.

## Key Considerations

If the path points to a directory, it will scan the entire directory for any modifications. Note that Cargo does not clean or reset OUT_DIR between builds. The contents of this directory may persist across rebuilds, even if the build script is re-run. This behavior is intentional to support incremental builds, such as native code compilation.

The search results also reveal there have been some reported issues with `rerun-if-changed` behavior in recent Rust versions, though these appear to be edge cases related to changing which directives are emitted between runs rather than problems with unchanged file content detection.

content.text:
Short answer: **Yes, a datestamp (mtime) change alone will force a rebuild, even if the file content is byte-identical.** Cargo's default change detection keys on the filesystem mtime, not a content hash.

## What this means for your codegen-before-build setup

If your generator unconditionally writes the file every run, you bump its mtime every build → Cargo rebuilds anything that depends on it (the crate compile if it's a source file; the `build.rs` re-run if it's tracked via `rerun-if-changed`). It doesn't read the bytes to notice they're unchanged.

There's been a long-standing desire to fingerprint by content — the `-Zhash-tracking` unstable flag (content hash + filesize as a fallback when mtime check fails) exists, and PR #8623 was a WIP toward it — but it is **not** the stable default. On stable Cargo, mtime rules.

## The fix: make your generator write-only-on-change

This is the standard, robust pattern. Have the codegen compute the new content in memory, compare it against the existing file, and **skip the write (or restore the old mtime) if identical**:

```rust
let new = generate();
let unchanged = fs::read_to_string(&path).map(|old| old == new).unwrap_or(false);
if !unchanged {
    fs::write(&path, new)?;
}
```

A `cmp -s new old && skip || cp` in a shell wrapper does the same thing. This way an unchanged file keeps its old mtime and Cargo correctly skips the rebuild.

Two related notes:
- **If codegen runs inside `build.rs`:** emit `cargo::rerun-if-changed=` for the *inputs* of your generator (templates, schema, the generator binary), not the generated output. Then the script only re-runs when an actual input changes. Without any `rerun-if-changed`, `build.rs` runs on every build.
- **Generated file outside `OUT_DIR`:** if the generated source lives in your tree (not `OUT_DIR`) and is compiled as part of the crate, the write-only-on-change discipline is what protects you — Cargo will see the unchanged mtime and skip recompiling that crate.
- **Tooling option:** `cargo-hold` exists specifically to paper over mtime churn (it BLAKE3-hashes files and restores timestamps for unchanged ones), useful in CI/Docker where checkouts don't preserve mtimes. But for your own generator, write-on-change is simpler and addresses it at the source.

Sources:
- [Build Scripts — The Cargo Book](https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/reference/build-scripts.html)
- [Fingerprint by file contents instead of mtime (#6529)](https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/issues/6529)
- [mtime+content tracking (PR #8623)](https://github.com/rust-lang/cargo/pull/8623)
- [cargo-hold — crates.io](https://crates.io/crates/cargo-hold)

aiTitle: Rust build caching with generated files

content: You asked whether running a codegen before every Rust build forces rebuilds. Answer: Cargo uses file mtime, not content, so rewriting a file always triggers a rebuild. Next: make your generator write only when content actually changes.
